title: Transporting Goods (Java / Python)
general_notes: No double deduction (except in point 5). Do not deduct again for an error already penalised in an earlier point. For example: if the prefix sum is built incorrectly, base the range sum computation marks on whether the student uses their own prefix array consistently.
This rubric applies to both Java and Python submissions. The Java method is calc(int[] a, int[][] b); the Python function is calc(a, b). Both are correct.
The algorithm must run in O(n) time. Solutions that recompute each range sum from scratch (O(n*q)) are not acceptable.

rubric_points.name: Prefix sum array allocation
rubric_points.target: An array C of size n is allocated and C[0] is initialised to A[0].

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No prefix sum array is allocated; the algorithm sums each query range from scratch using a nested loop, or returns a constant.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A prefix sum array is present but allocated with wrong size (e.g., n+1 allocated but C[0] not set to A[0], or size mismatch), or C[0] is not initialised separately from the loop.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: An array C of size n is allocated and C[0] = A[0] is set correctly before the cumulative loop begins.

rubric_points.name: Prefix sum construction
rubric_points.target: C[i] = A[i] + C[i-1] for i from 1 to n-1, building the cumulative sum array.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No prefix sum loop; C is either left as zeros or just copies A without accumulation.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A loop is present but the accumulation is wrong: e.g., C[i] = A[i] (copies without summing previous), or C[i] = C[i-1] (ignores current element), or iterates over the wrong range.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: C[i] = A[i] + C[i-1] for i from 1 to n-1, correctly building the cumulative sum so that C[j] = sum of A[0..j].

rubric_points.name: Output array allocation and query loop
rubric_points.target: An output array D of size n is allocated and a loop iterates over all n queries in B.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No query loop; only a single query is answered, or no output array is constructed.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A query loop is present but iterates over the wrong range (e.g., stops before the last query), or the output array has the wrong size.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: An output array D of size n is allocated and a loop iterates i from 0 to n-1, computing each query result.

rubric_points.name: Range sum computation with l=0 edge case
rubric_points.target: For each query (l, r): D[i] = C[r] - C[l-1] when l > 0; D[i] = C[r] when l == 0.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: D[i] does not use the prefix array; range is summed from scratch, or D[i] = C[r] - C[l] (off-by-one, using l instead of l-1).

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: D[i] = C[r] - C[l-1] is used correctly when l > 0, but the l == 0 edge case is not handled: accessing C[-1] causes an index error or the formula is applied unconditionally.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: When l > 0: D[i] = C[r] - C[l-1]; when l == 0: D[i] = C[r]. Both cases correctly compute the closed-interval sum A[l..r].

rubric_points.name: Overall correctness
rubric_points.target: calc returns the correct list of range sums for all queries in O(n) time, without forbidden keywords.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Returns incorrect sums for most queries, or contains forbidden keywords ('check_calc', 'eval').

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Returns correct sums when l > 0 but crashes or gives wrong results when l == 0, or the algorithm is O(n*q) due to per-query iteration over A.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Returns the correct range sum list for all queries including l == 0 in O(n) time; forbidden keywords are absent.

buckets.label: Internally consistent but incorrect final result
buckets.description: Logic follows consistently from the student's own prefix sum construction, but the result is systematically off (e.g., always off by one at query boundaries or consistently missing one element).
